title: श्री गुरु स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री गुरु स्तोत्रम् ॥

गुरुर्ब्रह्मा गुरुर्विष्णुः गुरुर्देवो महेश्वरः।
गुरुः साक्षात् परं ब्रह्म तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥१॥

अखण्डमण्डलाकारं व्याप्तं येन चराचरम्।
तत्पदं दर्शितं येन तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥२॥

अज्ञानतिमिरान्धस्य ज्ञानाञ्जनशलाकया।
चक्षुरुन्मीलितं येन तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥३॥

चैतन्यः शाश्वतः शान्तो व्योमातीतो निरञ्जनः।
बिन्दुनादकलातीतः तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥४॥

ज्ञानशक्तिसमारूढस्तत्त्वमालाविभूषितः।
भुक्तिमुक्तिप्रदाता च तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥५॥

अनेकजन्मसम्प्राप्तकर्मबन्धविदाहिने।
आत्मज्ञानप्रदानेन तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥६॥

शोषणं भवसिन्धोश्च ज्ञापनं सारसम्पदः।
गुरोः पादोदकं सम्यक् तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥७॥

न गुरोरधिकं तत्त्वं न गुरोरधिकं तपः।
तत्त्वज्ञानात्परं नास्ति तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥८॥

मन्नाथः श्रीजगन्नाथो मद्गुरुः श्रीजगद्गुरुः।
मदात्मा सर्वभूतात्मा तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥९॥

गुरुरादिरनादिश्च गुरुः परमदैवतम्।
गुरोः परतरं नास्ति तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥१०॥

त्वमेव माता च पिता त्वमेव
त्वमेव बन्धुश्च सखा त्वमेव।
त्वमेव विद्या द्रविणं त्वमेव
त्वमेव सर्वं मम देव देव॥११॥

॥ इति श्रीगुरुस्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Guru Stotram ||

Gururbrahmaa Gururvishnuh Gururdevo Maheshvarah.
Guruh Saakshat Param Brahma Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||1||

Akhandamandalaakaaram Vyaaptam Yena Charaacharam.
Tatpadam Darshitam Yena Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||2||

Ajnaanatimirandasya Jnaanaanjanashaalaakayaa.
Chakshurunmeelitam Yena Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||3||

Chaitanyah Shaashvatah Shaanto Vyomaateeto Niranajanah.
Bindunadekaalaateetah Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||4||

Jnaanashaktisamaaroodhah Tattvamalaavibhooshitah.
Bhuktimuktipradaata Cha Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||5||

Anekajanmasampraptakarmabandhavidaahine.
Aatmajnaanapradaanena Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||6||

Shoshanam Bhavasindhooshcha Jnaapanam Saarasampadah.
Guroh Paadodakam Samyak Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||7||

Na Guroradhikam Tattvam Na Guroradhikam Tapah.
Tattvajnaanaat Param Naasti Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||8||

Mannaathah Shrijagannathoh Madguruh Shrijagadguruh.
Madaatmaa Sarvabhootaatmaa Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||9||

Gururaadiranaadishcha Guruh Paramadaivatam.
Guroh Parataram Naasti Tasmai Shrigurave Namah.||10||

Tvameva Maataa Cha Pitaa Tvameva
Tvameva Bandhushcha Sakhaa Tvameva.
Tvameva Vidyaa Dravinam Tvameva
Tvameva Sarvam Mama Deva Deva.||11||

|| Iti Shrigurustotram Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning:
(1) गुरु ब्रह्मा हैं, गुरु विष्णु हैं, गुरु ही देव महेश्वर हैं। गुरु साक्षात् परब्रह्म हैं, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(2) जिन्होंने अखण्ड मण्डलाकार चराचर जगत् में व्याप्त उस तत्व (ब्रह्म) का दर्शन कराया, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(3) अज्ञान रूपी अन्धकार से अन्धे हुए मेरे नेत्रों को ज्ञान रूपी अञ्जन शलाका से जिन्होंने खोला, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(4) जो चैतन्य, शाश्वत, शान्त, आकाश से भी परे, निरञ्जन, बिन्दु-नाद-कला से अतीत हैं, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(5) ज्ञानशक्ति पर आरूढ़, तत्त्वों की माला से विभूषित, भुक्ति (भोग) और मुक्ति दोनों प्रदान करने वाले श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(6) अनेक जन्मों में संचित कर्मबन्धनों को आत्मज्ञान प्रदान करके जलाने वाले श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(7) गुरु के चरणोदक (चरण-जल) भवसागर का शोषण करने और सार सम्पदा (सत्य ज्ञान) का ज्ञान कराने वाले हैं, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(8) गुरु से बढ़कर कोई तत्त्व नहीं, गुरु से बढ़कर कोई तपस्या नहीं, तत्त्वज्ञान से परे कुछ नहीं, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(9) मेरे नाथ श्री जगन्नाथ हैं, मेरे गुरु श्री जगद्गुरु हैं, मेरी आत्मा सर्वभूतात्मा है, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(10) गुरु आदि हैं और अनादि भी, गुरु परम दैवत हैं, गुरु से परे कुछ नहीं है, उन श्रीगुरु को नमस्कार।

(11) आप ही माता हैं, आप ही पिता हैं, आप ही बन्धु और सखा हैं, आप ही विद्या और धन हैं, आप ही मेरे सर्वस्व हैं, हे देवदेव!
benefits: Supreme prayer honoring the Guru as Brahma, Vishnu, and Maheshwara
Opens the inner eye of wisdom and removes spiritual ignorance
Burns karmic bonds accumulated over many lifetimes
Invokes the blessing of both material enjoyment (bhukti) and liberation (mukti)
Establishes the highest devotion and surrender to the Guru
Most sacred prayer for Guru Purnima and Thursday worship
Leads to realization of the ultimate truth (Param Brahma)
description: Guru Stotram (श्री गुरु स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise dedicated to Lord Dattatreya. Attributed to Guru Gita - Skanda Purana, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Devotees recite this prayer to supreme prayer honoring the Guru as Brahma, Vishnu, and Maheshwara and open the inner eye of wisdom and remove spiritual ignorance. It is also said to lead to realization of the ultimate truth (Param Brahma). This prayer is traditionally recited on Thursday (Guruvar) and during Guru Purnima to invoke divine grace. The text contains 11 verses and takes around 6 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Guru Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Guru Stotram (Gurur Brahma Gurur Vishnu) in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Sacred hymn honoring the Guru as the supreme divine for spiritual awakening.
alsoKnownAs: Tvameva Mata cha Pita Tvameva Stotra

seoFaqs.question: What is the Guru Stotram and which deity does it praise?
seoFaqs.answer: The Guru Stotram is the universal Sanskrit hymn praising the Guru as the supreme Brahman. The most famous verse — Gurur Brahma Gurur Vishnuh Gururdevo Maheshvarah, Guruh Sakshat Param Brahma Tasmai Sri Gurave Namah — is recited as the opening salutation before every Hindu spiritual practice. The full eleven-verse stotra (drawn from the Guru Gita section of the Skanda Purana, in dialogue between Shiva and Parvati) salutes the Guru as the unified Trimurti, the one who points out the all-pervading Akhanda-mandala-kara Brahman, the surgeon who opens the disciple's wisdom-eye with the collyrium-stick of jnana, the eternal Chaitanya beyond bindu-nada-kala, the giver of both bhukti (worldly enjoyment) and mukti (liberation), and the burner of karmic bonds across many lifetimes. (Sources: Skanda Purana Guru Gita; Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia Guru.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the verse 'Gurur Brahma Gurur Vishnuh' mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse declares: 'The Guru is Brahma (the creator), the Guru is Vishnu (the preserver), the Guru is Maheshvara (the dissolver) — the Guru himself is verily the supreme Brahman; salutations to that Guru.' This identifies the Guru with the cosmic Trimurti and ultimately with the absolute Para-Brahman. The verse teaches that the human Guru is not a mere teacher but the very embodiment of the divine creative-sustaining-dissolving consciousness through whom the disciple's spiritual journey unfolds. It is recited as the universal mangala-charana (auspicious opening) at the start of every Vedic study session, every dance/music/yoga class, every spiritual lecture, and every formal puja. The verse encapsulates the entire Hindu Guru-tattva philosophy. (Sources: Skanda Purana Guru Gita 1.39; Hindupedia; Sandhya Bhashya.)

seoFaqs.question: What does 'Tvameva Mata cha Pita Tvameva' mean and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing eleventh verse — Tvameva mata cha pita tvameva, Tvameva bandhushcha sakha tvameva, Tvameva vidya dravinam tvameva, Tvameva sarvam mama deva deva — means 'You alone are my mother and father, you alone are my brother and friend, you alone are my knowledge and wealth, you alone are everything to me, O God of gods.' This verse, traditionally attributed to the same Guru Gita corpus and to Pandava Pitamaha Bhishma in the Mahabharata, is the supreme verse of total surrender (sharanagati) used by devotees across all Hindu traditions. Many recite it as their prarthana before sleep or as a vyakti-gata samarpan to their ishta-deva or Guru. It is sometimes recited as a stand-alone prayer outside the Guru Stotram. (Sources: Mahabharata; Skanda Purana Guru Gita; Hindupedia.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should Guru Stotram be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Thursday (Guruvar) is the prescribed weekday since it is named after Brihaspati, the deva-guru. The supreme occasion is Guru Purnima — Ashadha Shukla Purnima (July full moon) — celebrated as Vyasa Jayanti, the birth of Maharshi Veda Vyasa who systematised the Vedas. Other powerful occasions are the birthday of one's own Guru, the deeksha-divasa (initiation anniversary), and the start of any new spiritual practice. Daily recitation is recommended at brahma-muhurta facing east, ideally before any other japa or stotra-paatha — the Guru Stotram is the universal mangala-charana that opens every Hindu sadhana. The verses are also chanted by students before exams, by performing artists before stage, and by yogis before asana practice. (Sources: Drikpanchang Guru Purnima; Skanda Purana; Hindupedia Guru.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Guru Stotram grant?
seoFaqs.answer: The hymn invokes the supreme grace of the Guru, who alone can open the inner wisdom-eye (verse 3 — Ajnana-timirandhasya jnananjana-shalakaya, chakshur-unmilitam yena), grant the realisation of the all-pervading Akhanda-mandala-kara Brahman (verse 2), bestow both bhukti and mukti (verse 5 — Bhukti-mukti-pradata), burn karmic bonds across many lifetimes (verse 6 — Aneka-janma-sampraprata-karma-bandha-vidahine), reveal that nothing is higher than tattva-jnana (verse 8), and ultimately establish the disciple's identity with the supreme self (verse 11 — Tvameva sarvam mama deva deva). The hymn is considered the master-mantra for spiritual awakening — without Guru-kripa, no sadhana bears fruit. (Sources: Skanda Purana phalashruti; Hindupedia Guru-tattva; Stotra Nidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of 'Akhanda Mandalakaram' verse?
seoFaqs.answer: The second verse — Akhanda-mandala-akaram vyaptam yena characharam, Tatpadam darshitam yena tasmai sri gurave namah — means 'Salutations to that Guru by whom was shown that ultimate state (tat padam) which has the form of the unbroken-circular-totality (akhanda-mandala-akara) and which pervades all moving and unmoving entities (charachara).' This is the Vedantic Brahma-darshana — the Guru's supreme gift is to point out the disciple's own true nature as the all-pervading consciousness. The 'akhanda mandala akara' image describes Brahman as a limitless, undivided sphere/circle that contains everything within it. The verse teaches that the Guru's role is not to give something new but to reveal what was always present — the disciple's identity with the cosmic All. (Sources: Skanda Purana Guru Gita; Adi Shankara Brahma-Sutra Bhashya; Hindupedia.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Guru Stotram differ from Guru Paduka Stotram and Guru Gita?
seoFaqs.answer: All three belong to Guru-bhakti corpus but vary in scope. The Guru Stotram (11 verses, from Skanda Purana Guru Gita) is the universal Guru-vandana — the most-recited Guru praise in pan-Indian Hindu practice, suitable as opening invocation for any practice. The Guru Paduka Stotram (9 verses, by Adi Shankara) is a focused devotional meditation on the Guru's sandals as the boat across samsara — used by serious sadhakas in Sri Vidya and Advaita lineages. The Guru Gita is the much larger 352-verse Sanskrit text from the Skanda Purana (Uttara Khanda), recited as parayana — read as a complete scripture during Guru Purnima fortnight or as continuous Saptaha (seven-day reading). The shorter Guru Stotram is essentially a digest of the most essential verses from the Guru Gita. (Sources: Skanda Purana Guru Gita; Adi Shankara stotras; Stotra Nidhi.)
